name: Three Sacred Treasures
shortDescription: The Three Sacred Treasures (Sanshu no Jingi) are the imperial regalia of Japan, symbolizing the emperor's legitimacy and divine authority.
description:
The Three Sacred Treasures, or Sanshu no Jingi (三種の神器), are a set of three items that traditionally symbolize the Emperor of Japan and his divine right to rule. They are the mirror (Yata no Kagami), the sword (Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi), and the jewel (Yasakani no Magatama). These items are deeply rooted in Japanese mythology and hold immense cultural and spiritual significance. According to legend, these treasures were originally given to Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu, when he descended from the heavens to rule Japan. The mirror, Yata no Kagami, is said to embody wisdom and honesty. It is enshrined in Ise Grand Shrine, dedicated to Amaterasu. The sword, Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, represents valor and is associated with the god Susanoo, who found it inside the body of the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. It is traditionally kept at Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, though its presence there is debated. The jewel, Yasakani no Magatama, symbolizes benevolence and is believed to be kept at the Imperial Palace in Tokyo. 

The treasures are not just material objects; they represent core virtues and principles of Japanese leadership. They embody the emperor's role as a benevolent ruler, a wise leader, and a courageous protector of the nation. The transfer of these treasures is a key part of the imperial enthronement ceremony, signifying the transfer of power and legitimacy to the new emperor. Because of their sacred nature and connection to the imperial family, the treasures are rarely seen in public, and their exact appearance remains shrouded in mystery. Their importance transcends their physical form, representing the continuity of the imperial line and the enduring values of Japanese culture. The concept is related to Shinto beliefs of purity and the divine connection of the emperor to the gods.

sources.title: A Cultural History of Japanese Mythology
sources.author: Ueda, Masaaki
